coredata.dc:title: pH-sensitive ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@G hybrid microspheres; Synthesis, characterization and drug delivery application
coredata.dc:creator: Pooresmaeil, Malihe
Namazi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a simple approach used to develop new ternary gelatin-coated magnetite/graphene quantum dots hybrid microspheres (Fe3O4/GQDs@GM) as an anticancer drug carrier. Moreover, a neat gelatin microsphere (GM) was prepared to compare the performance of both prepared systems. Prepared samples were identified by using fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis. photoluminescence (PL) analysis approved the PL property for the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, as well as, the vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) exhibited superparamagnetic properties for it. Study the in vitro biodegradation behavior of ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@GM in the trypsin approved its enzymatic degradation capability. 30.15% and 22.29% of curcumin (CUR) as a natural anticancer drug respectively was loaded in Fe3O4/GQDs@GM and GM. The drug release test at two different pH (7.4 and 5.0) showed the pH-sensitive drug release pattern. The biocompatibility of the prepared microspheres was confirmed by calculation the viability of the breast cancer cell (MDA-MB 231) after treatment with each microsphere. DAPI staining test revealed apoptosis on MDA-MB 231 cancer cells that treated with the drug-loaded microspheres. The results of the drug delivery and biological studies revealed an improved performance for ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, therefore, it could be proposed as a biodegradable, pH-sensitive, and non-toxic vehicle for anticancer drug delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ternary hybrid
Magnetite
Graphene quantum dots
Gelatin
Drug delivery

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@G hybrid microsphere

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM fabrication.
FT-IR spectra for the Fe3O4, GQDs, Fe3O4/GQDs, GM, and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM.
EDX spectrum of (a) Fe3O4/GQDs, (b) Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, and (c) EDX cross-section map of Fe3O4/GQDs@GM.
The XRD patterns of Fe3O4, GQDs, Fe3O4/GQDs, GM, and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM.
SEM image of the Fe3O4/GQDs, GM, Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM cross-section image.
(a) PL spectra of the GQDs, Fe3O4/GQDs, Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, and (b) Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM VSM magnetization curve.
Degradation profile of the GM and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM microspheres using trypsin (PBS, 37 °C).
(a) Values of the loaded CUR (%), and (b) cumulative in vitro release profiles of CUR at 37 °C from the GM@CUR and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM@CUR microspheres in (pH 7.4 and pH 5.0.

Cell growth inhibition rates by different concentrations of (a) GM and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, (b) CUR, GM@CUR, and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM@CUR (b) after 24 h incubation on MDA-MB 231 cell line. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3).
Fluorescence microscopic images of MDA-MB 231 without treatment (control) and after treatment with GM, Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, CUR, GM@CUR, Fe3O4/GQDs@GM@CUR.

head.ce:title: pH-sensitive ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@G hybrid microspheres; Synthesis, characterization and drug delivery application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Research Laboratory, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polymer Research Laboratory, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author Polymer Research Laboratory, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a simple approach used to develop new ternary gelatin-coated magnetite/graphene quantum dots hybrid microspheres (Fe3O4/GQDs@GM) as an anticancer drug carrier. Moreover, a neat gelatin microsphere (GM) was prepared to compare the performance of both prepared systems. Prepared samples were identified by using fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis. photoluminescence (PL) analysis approved the PL property for the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, as well as, the vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) exhibited superparamagnetic properties for it. Study the in vitro biodegradation behavior of ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@GM in the trypsin approved its enzymatic degradation capability. 30.15% and 22.29% of curcumin (CUR) as a natural anticancer drug respectively was loaded in Fe3O4/GQDs@GM and GM. The drug release test at two different pH (7.4 and 5.0) showed the pH-sensitive drug release pattern. The biocompatibility of the prepared microspheres was confirmed by calculation the viability of the breast cancer cell (MDA-MB 231) after treatment with each microsphere. DAPI staining test revealed apoptosis on MDA-MB 231 cancer cells that treated with the drug-loaded microspheres. The results of the drug delivery and biological studies revealed an improved performance for ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, therefore, it could be proposed as a biodegradable, pH-sensitive, and non-toxic vehicle for anticancer drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the research fields of medicine have been revolutionized via the development of bio-based controlled-release systems owing to their improved retention (EPR) effect and permeability, reduced side effects of the drug, prolonged circulation time, and enhanced drug bioavailability [1–4]. Because of these advantages, the controlled drug release systems (DDSs) have received great interest in medicine [5–9]. The significant required features for controlled DDSs are inertness, biocompatibility, and the capability to realize a high loading of a drug for the required blood levels [10–12]. In fact, the managing of the drug release behavior to keep the dosage of the drug for a long period at a best-required level or avoid from the burst release of drug effectively improve the efficacy of the bio-active agents at the ideal level [13–15]. Up to now, several systems have been suggested as controlled delivery systems that microparticulate DDSs are one of them.

Generally, microparticulate DDSs are attained from natural or synthetic polymeric materials. Among the natural polymeric materials, microparticulate gelatin for numerous aspects in the biomedical field particularly in DDSs could be a great candidate because of its unique characteristics like pH-sensitivity, hydrophilicity, economically, non-toxicity, degradability, and biocompatibility [16–18]. So, recently the gelatin-based microspheres were prepared and investigated as a carrier for delivery of a wide range of anticancer drugs like doxorubicin, methotrexate, 5-fluorouracil, and paclitaxel [19–22]. In general, GM is shown as a suitable platform for the controlled delivery of various drugs and long-acting biodegradable capability [23–26]. For the preparation of GM, emulsification-coacervation in water-in-oil (W/O) emulsions is the most common route [27–29]. In regards to DDSs, the chemical crosslinking of polymeric chains has been estimated to control the fast dissolution of gelatin; therefore, it can improve a sustained release of the loaded drug. For the crosslinking of gelatin and preparing of an insoluble product different aldehydes, for example, formaldehyde [30,31] and glutaraldehyde [32,33] could be employed. These positive features of the GM could be more improved by incorporating nanoparticles (NPs). Magnetite, graphene oxide, GQDs, layered double hydroxides (LDHs), and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are some of the commonly used NPs for the production of the systems with improved medicinal using capability [34–38]. As an example, in 2020 it was shown that the introduction of the LDH(Cu/Al) in the network of the carboxymethyl cellulose enhances its drug loading amount and its release behavior [39].

By considering various NPs, the magnetic nanomaterials have attracted great interest in numerous research fields, for example, medicine, biology, material science, and physics [40–42]. Magnetic NPs exhibit superparamagnetic property, high surface area, great biocompatibility, and low toxicity [43–45]. The superparamagnetic iron oxide NPs in comparison with other magnetic materials (Fe, Co, Ni) [46–49] have been generally employed in various biomedical platforms, such as in-vivo drug delivery [50], in-vitro cell separation [51], tumor hyperthermia [52], and gene delivery [53]. Interestingly, in the presence of an external magnetic source, nanocarriers based on iron oxide magnetic materials can deliver the drug at the specific targeted tissue [54]. Hence, by introducing the magnetite NPs (MNPs) in the drug delivery systems the systemic adverse effects decrease through the fixing of drug carriers in the desired site of the body [55]. Che el al. prepared the targeted drug delivery vehicle with core/shell structural; magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MMSNs) as core and the gelatin layer as the shell. This research group showed that the prepared system could facilely respond to an external magnet and release the paclitaxel in a sustained manner [56].

The magnetite NPs could be hybridized with other particles like quantum dots (QDs) to obtain the new hybrid with the advantage of both components. Selenium QDs, GQDs, silver QDs, silicon QDs, and carbon Ds (CDs) are some of the investigated QDs for medicinal applications. Among them, today’s, the carbon-based QDs have received the main position as drug carriers because of their biocompatibility, possessing the -OH and -COOH functional groups on their surface that create the sites for functionalization with other molecules, needing to the lower cost for synthesis, and preparation via easy techniques. In comparison with CDs, the GQDs have high crystallinity, excellent fluorescence, and large surface area. In the case of structure, the CDs are spherical particles mainly with SP3 hybridization while the GQDs are single-layered and disc-shaped with SP2 hybridized carbon having graphene lattice inside the dots [57–59]. By considering the above facts, the combination of the magnetite NPs with GQDs can induce the fluorescence property to the system, enhance the number of its hydroxyl groups, as well as, introduced the carboxylic functional groups and conjugated aromatic rings to the nanohybrid structure that could be more valuable for loading a wide range of anticancer drugs. For example, the results of the study of the GQD-ConA@Fe3O4 performance as a drug carrier exhibited that the nanocomposites can serve as the multifunctional vehicle for cancer cell therapeutic and diagnostic applications [60].

GQDs are newly introduced photoluminescent nanomaterials that could be used to induces this property to the designed system [45,61,62]. Up today the enormous efforts are dedicated to the synthesis of GQDs (top-down and bottom-up routes) and their properties [63]. Delocalized electrons in large surface area, chemical stability, solubility in different solvents, easy alterability of size, and π-π interaction capability by various drugs are excellent properties of these materials that led to their use in drug delivery. Because of the mentioned features, GQDs could be used as talented materials for biomedical applications; cellular imaging, and drug delivery [64–66]. So, the survey of the published literature approves its efficiency as a drug delivery system [15,67,68].

By considering the above facts and extension of our ongoing efforts toward the development of innovative DDSs with controlled drug release behavior [69], in this study, we organized a developing method to accomplish a new biosystem comprised of the Fe3O4, GQDs, and gelatin. CUR as a model of the anticancerous drug was loaded into the prepared biosystems. The drug release profiles were investigated at 37 °C and two different pH; 7.4 and 5.0 (Simulated with the pH of normal cell and tumor cell respectively). The results of the drug release and biological studies revealed that the ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@GM has improved performance compared to the neat GM. Therefore, it can be a suitable carrier for anticancer drug delivery.

Citric acid (CA), sodium hydroxide, gelatin, formaldehyde (FA, 37 wt%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and acetone were obtained from Merck Co. (Germany). Ammonia solution (25 wt%), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O), CUR, ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2.4H2O), 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), and 3–(4, 5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich Co. All solvents for experiments were used in analytical grade without further purification. Bidistilled water was used in all steps of this work. All of the other applied biological materials were achieved from GIBCO, USA.

Ultraviolet-Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) was performed with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 1700 Model, Japan). The FT-IR spectra were acquired with an FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Instruments, model Aquinox 55, Germany) as KBr pellets. SEM images and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) spectrum was attained by a TESCANMIRA3 scanning electron microscope operating at 5 kV. XRD patterns were documented by Siemens diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation at 35 kV. Magnetization measurement was occurred by MDKF vibrating sample magnetometer (Meghnatis Daghigh Kavir. Co., Kashan, Iran). The PL property was examined by a spectrofluorometric (LS45, PerkinElmer). Fluorescence microscope (Olympus microscope Bh2-RFCA, Japan) was used to observe the DNA morphology of the cells in the DAPI staining test. PHS-3C pH meter was utilized for the pH adjustments.

According to our previous work [70] and by considering the little changes, MNPs were synthesized via a facile coprecipitation method. Briefly, FeCl2.4H2O aqueous solution (10 mL, 0.3 M) was prepared in the three-necked ballon, then the ferric chloride (10.0 mL, 0.6 M) was added to it at 80–85 °C. The ammonia solution (0.8 M) was added dropwise into the reaction medium. MNPs were precipitated as a result of the ammonia solution addition. After finishing the ammonia solution droplets, the reaction was continued for 20 min. By using the magnetic decantation the black precipitate was collected and washed thoroughly with water and then dried in a vacuum oven. All steps of the Fe3O4 synthesis was performed under argon atmosphere.

In a typical procedure, GQDs were prepared through direct pyrolysis of CA according to the previously published work with some modifications [23]. 2 g of CA was heated up to 200 °C in a 10 mL beaker using a heating mantle. The prepared orange liquid was neutralized after about 30 min via dropwise addition to the ethanolic NaOH solution with 10 mg/mL concentration under vigorous stirring. The synthesized GQDs were kept at 4 °C and dark medium.

For the preparation of Fe3O4/GQDs, Fe3O4 (0.25 g) was dispersed in the GQDs solution containing 0.25 g of GQDs and then sonicated for 20 min using a bath sonicator. In the following, the mixture was refluxed under argon atmosphere for 3 h. Finally, the precipitate was separated with an external magnetic field and then washed with ethanol several times. The formed Fe3O4/GQDs nanohybrid dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C.

GM was fabricated via a single water-in-oil emulsion (W/O) method. In this regard, a 10% (w/v) gelatin stock solution (100 mL) was prepared from dissolving the gelatin (1 g) in deionized water at 55 °C for 2 h. The prepared gelatin solution was added dropwise to olive oil at 60 °C under magnetic stirring. The stirring was continued for 30 min. As the W/O emulsion was obtained, the temperature of the reaction medium was lowered to 4 °C through the rapid cooling in a water bath containing ice and stirred for 1 h. The 20 mL of pre-cooled acetone (4 °C) was added to the reaction mixture and stirring was continued for 15 min. To chemical crosslinking of the formed GM, FA solution was added dropwise to the emulsion. After stirring for 15 min, the GM was separated by using the centrifuge and washed several times with cooled acetone (4 °C) to remove residual oil and excess free cross-linker. Finally, the prepared GM was dried in the vacuum and stored in a closely packed glass vial.
Fe3O4/GQDs@GM was prepared in the rout as well as the GM preparation, but, at first Fe3O4/GQDs (0.15 g) was dispersed for 30 min in water (100 mL) then gelatin was dissolved in it.

The gravimetric method was used to investigate the enzymatic degradation capability of the prepared microspheres. Briefly, for each designated time point, the microspheres were poured in the separate centrifuge tube containing 10 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and allowed to reach equilibrium swelling. Subsequently, PBS was drained from the tubs and 4 mL of trypsin solution (50 μg/mL) was added to each tube. The set was shaken at a shaking speed of 100 rpm and at 37 °C. In the specific time, microspheres were taken out, rinsed with deionized water, and then with ethanol. In the end, microspheres were dried in vacuum, and the whole weight of microspheres and centrifuge tubes recorded as Wt. The remaining weight percentage was calculated by using Eq. (1).
(1)
R e m a i n i n g
w e i g h t
( % )
=
W t
W 0
× 100
where W0 and Wt denote the initial weight of each tube having microspheres and the remaining weight of each tube having degraded microspheres after studied time.

For CUR loading purpose, firstly drug solution was prepared through the CUR dissolving in ethanol. 100 mg of each carrier was suspended in drug solution (500 ppm, ethanol). The mixture was shacked for 48 h in dark condition. CUR-loaded microspheres washed two times with ethanol to eliminate physically adsorbed CUR from the surface and finally dried in the vacuum. To measure the drug loading content in the microspheres, the drug loading solution was collected after the loading process and the amount of free drug was determined by using the UV-Vis spectroscopy. Eq. (2) and Eq. (3) were used to calculate the loading percentage of the CUR.
(2)
C U R
l o a d i n g
c a p a c i t y
(
% w
)
=
A m o u n t
o f
C U R
i n
m i c r o s p h e r e
A m o u n t
o f
m i c r o s p h e r e
x
100
(3)
C U R
e n t r a p m e n t
e f f i c i e n c y

)

C U R
i n
m i c r o s p h e r e

C U R
f e d
i n i t i a l l y
x
100

Specific amounts of microspheres were dispersed in a 10 mL of buffer solution (pH 7.4, and pH 5.0) having ethanol at 37 °C. At regular time intervals, certain amounts of solution were sampled and in order to keep the volume of solution constant, the equal amount of fresh buffer was readded. The amount of released CUR was determined using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer, subsequent comparison with the calibration curve of CUR, and finally using Eq. (4):
(4)
C U R
r e l e a s e =
T h e
a m o u n t
o f
r e l e a s e d
C U R
T h e
a m o u n t
o f
l o a d e d
C U R
× 100

The cytotoxicity of the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM and GM without and with CUR was evaluated by using the MTT assay on the human breast cancer cell line (MDA-MB 231 cells). Typically, MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded into 96 well plates at a density of 10,000 cells per well and incubated for 24 h under 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C to attach the bottom of wells. In the following, the cells were treated with different concentrations of GM, Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, CUR, GM@CUR, and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM@CUR and then incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The untreated cells were considered as control. After 24 h, the medium was removed and cells rinsed with PBS. In the following, the MTT solution (200 μL, 0.5 mg mL−1) was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C. The MTT solution was drained after 4 h incubation and the formed formazan crystals were dissolved via the addition of DMSO (200 μL). In the end, the solution absorbance was measured at 570 nm by using the ELISA plate reader to calculate the cell viability percentage.

For assessing the variation of the nucleus morphology and their density as a result of treatment the morphology of the treated MDA-MB 231 cells was pictured after 24 h incubation in the presence of the GM, Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, CUR, GM@CUR, and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM@CUR. First, MDA-MB 231 cells were seeded on sterile glass coverslips in six-well plates at a concentration of 250,000 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. In the next step, the seeded MDA-MB 231 cells in six-well plates having coverslips were treated with samples and incubated at 37 °C. The untreated cells were considered as control. After 24 h, the cells were fixed with paraformaldehyde (3 wt%) after washing with PBS. In the following, the excess of the paraformaldehyde was discarded and cells washed with PBS (three times). Incubation with Triton X-100 (0.1% w/v) provided the possibility of the nucleus staining with DAPI. In the end, by using a fluorescence microscope the morphology of the DNA was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@G hybrid microsphere
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a newly pH-responsive nanocarrier was prepared and then its efficiency as CUR delivery bio-system was evaluated. The sequential steps of the ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@G hybrid microsphere preparation are presented in Fig. 1 . In the first step, Fe3O4 and GQDs were synthesized separately and then hybridized in easy rout [71]. Afterward, the gelatin encapsulated Fe3O4/GQDs was obtained by dropping a mixture of the aqueous dispersion of Fe3O4/GQDs and gelatin into oil solution (W/O emulsion). Finally, the crosslinked microsphere was obtained via the crosslinking with FA as a chemical crosslinker.

The FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4, GQDs, Fe3O4/GQDs, GM, and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM are shown in Fig. 2 . The typical characteristic peak of Fe-O in the Fe3O4 spectrum appeared as a centered absorption band at 588 cm−1 which revealed the successful synthesis of Fe3O4 NPs. For the GQDs, the strong and broad peaks at 3248 cm−1 can be assigned to the characteristic peak of the O-H stretching. The strong, paired peaks at 1393 cm−1 and 1600-1800 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching frequency of carboxylic groups which merged with stretching vibration of C=C, respectively. After the formation of the Fe3O4/GQDs, all the characteristic peaks of GQDs and the Fe3O4 are observed in the Fe3O4/GQDs spectrum, indicating that the Fe3O4 were successfully hybridized with GQDs. The GM FT-IR spectrum displayed the bands at 3374 cm−1 and 1692 cm−1 that respectively are assigned to the N-H and the C=O stretching vibrations. The absorption band at 1529 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of amid II in the gelatin structure. The FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4/GQDs@GM is a mathematical combination of Fe3O4/GQDs and GM spectra having the characteristic peaks of both materials. This observation could support the formation of the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM nanocomposite microsphere.

The successful formation of the Fe3O4/GQDs NPs with a good purity was investigated by using the EDX spectrum. The obtained spectrum presented the presence of C, O, and Fe elements (Fig. 3 a) in Fe3O4/GQDs structure. The appearance of these elements is a witness for the hybridization of the Fe3O4 and GQDs. In additionally EDX analysis was used to investigate the distribution of Fe3O4/GQDs NPs in the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM structure. The obtained EDX spectrum and cross-section EDX map are shown in Fig. 3b and c and revealed the existence of C, O, and, Fe elements in Fe3O4/GQDs@GM structure. The existence of Fe element in the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM matrix could be good evidence for the dispersion of the Fe3O4/GQDs in the microsphere matrix. Moreover, from the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM EDX map (Fig. 3c), the good distribution of the Fe3O4/GQDs in the nanocomposite structure is detectable.

The crystalline properties of the synthesized materials were studied by using the X-ray diffraction analysis. The XRD patterns of Fe3O4, GQDs, Fe3O4/GQDs, GM, and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM in the 2θ range of 5–70° are presented in Fig. 4 . XRD pattern for the Fe3O4 shows the six characteristic peaks at 2θ 30.35°, 35.95°, 43.45°, 53.70°, 57.25°, and 62.88° which signify the formation of MNPs. The observed peaks correspond to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) planes [70]. GQDs displayed a wide peak at 2θ 24° (002) that assigned to the hexagonal lattice structure. Bragg equation obtained the value of the interlayer spacing about 3.7 Å that is similar to the reported value for the graphitic materials and could exhibit the GQDs formation via the pyrolysis of CA [23]. The XRD pattern of the Fe3O4/GQDs is similar to the Fe3O4 NPs that shows that the hybridization process does not alter the crystalline nature of Fe3O4 NPs. The broad detected peak at 2θ 16-25° in the XRD patterns of the GM is related to its amorph nature. The XRD pattern of the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM has the peaks of the Fe3O4/GQDs (with lower intensity) and one wide peak at 2θ 16-26 that arises from the successful encapsulation of the Fe3O4/GQDs in the amorph network of gelatin [56]. The observed patterns indicate the successful synthesis of Fe3O4/GQDs@GM.

SEM analysis was performed to study the morphology of the prepared Fe3O4/GQDs, GM, and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM from the size, and shape viewpoint. SEM image of the Fe3O4/GQDs is shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen, the formed Fe3O4/GQDs NPs have the spherical shape in the range of 20–30 nm that is larger than neat GQDs and Fe3O4 NPs that previously reported [72,73]. The SEM image of the GM and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM revealed the spherical shape and well dispersed of microspheres. By introducing the Fe3O4/GQDs to the polymer matrix the size and polydispersity of the microspheres were increased, as well as, its surface became rougher. Moreover, in the cross-section image of the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, the presence of Fe3O4/GQDs NPs within the microsphere can be seen that could be good evidence for the encapsulation of Fe3O4/GQDs via gelatin biopolymer. However, for DDS, this structure has a suitable affinity for the diffusion of water and PO4 3− into the microsphere and the drug release from it [2].

PL spectra for the GQDs, Fe3O4/GQDs, and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM was obtained after excitation at 360 nm under the UV light. GQDs revealed the emission peak at 460 nm that results from its special structure. For the Fe3O4/GQDs and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM, the PL intensity is lower than the neat GQDs, as well as, they showed a partial shift in the maximum wavelength compared to the neat GQDs. The filter effect, self-absorbing, external or internal transformations phenomena, and intersystem transitions could be reasons for the observations. The lower fluorescence intensity for Fe3O4/GQDs in comparison with Fe3O4/GQDs@GM could be due to aggregation that resulted in the greater extinction effect for Fe3O4/GQDs than other components. The results of the PL study displayed that the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM could be proposed as bioimaging vehicles for future studies. The magnetization curve of Fe3O4/GQDs@GM was determined at room temperature by VSM analysis and the obtained S-like curve is shown in Fig. 6 . The observed weakening in the saturation magnetization values for Fe3O4/GQDs@GM compare to the neat Fe3O4 NPs [70] indicates a successful encapsulation procedure as a microsphere. However, it could transfer the target site of the body by using a strong external magnetic field that is valuable in drug delivery science for reducing the side effects of treatment.

Biodegradability is frequently considered as the main factor in medicine, therefore, the degradability of the fabricated microspheres was tested upon enzyme exposure. In this work, trypsin as a model of the enzymes was used for evaluation of the degradation percentage of microspheres. The obtained profiles for microspheres degradation are shown in Fig. 7 . In the initial times, the microspheres degraded faster and the degradation rate decreased bypassing the time. From the results, during 72 h and at 37 °C respectively about 43% and 38% of degradation percentages were observed for the GM and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM in the presence of trypsin. The observed profiles result from the reduction of the organic weight of the samples and related to the presence of the gelatin as a degradable biological macromolecule in their structure. In fact the trypsin as a serine protease could cleave the peptide bonds that link amino acid residues. The higher degradation of GM in comparison with Fe3O4/GQDs@GM may be related to the interaction of the Fe3O4/GQDs with the gelatin molecule side chains to decrease the contact surface of the polymer for the enzyme degradation. The finding of this test shows the enzymatic biodegradability of the microspheres as a drug delivery bio-system and offers that the trypsin is the suitable enzyme for gelatin microsphere degradation [74–76].

CUR as a well-known natural-based drug with anticancer ability was selected to load in the prepared microspheres. UV–Vis spectroscopy was used to estimate the CUR loading values in the GM and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM; the obtained values are presented in Fig. 8 a. It has been detected that the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM has a higher drug loading value compared to GM. The higher drug loading percentage in the case of Fe3O4/GQDs@GM could be explained by more porosity in the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM (due to the Fe3O4/GQDs dispersion in its matrix), higher electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bond formation between polar groups of Fe3O4/GQDs@GM and CUR. Moreover, the π-π interaction between the conjugated aromatic rings of GQDs and the benzene ring of aromatic CUR could be another participating factor in the observed CUR values for the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM [77,78]. In the following, the CUR-release studies were assessed at 37 °C and under two different pH; 7.4, and pH 5.0; respectively simulated with the pH of normal and tumor cell. As can be seen, the CUR released in a sustained manner from constructed carriers. As shown in Fig. 8b, the value of the released CUR for both of the carriers in the simulated medium with tumor tissue environments is higher than the normal physiological condition that could be assigned to two phenomena; First, the rupture of the microspheres as breakage of formed imine bonds between gelatin and crosslinker in acidic medium and two, protonation of the amine group of the gelatin which leads to the microsphere extension and it provide the buffer penetration and high drug release. The increase in the CUR release of Fe3O4/GQDs@GM could be related to an increase in the porosity of the network, so, the CUR release rout opens and its release. The results of the drug delivery discovered an upgraded performance for Fe3O4/GQDs@GM in comparison with GM, hence it could be proposed as a hopeful candidate vehicle for CUR delivery.

In this work, the MTT assay against human breast cancer cells (MDA-MB 231) was performed to investigate the biocompatibility of the prepared systems for use as a new drug delivery agent. From the MTT results, it could be found that for both of the studied samples the cell viability decreased as concentration increased. As seen in Fig. 9 a, after 24 h incubation at a concentration range of 0–250 μg/mL both of the fabricated microspheres demonstrated good biocompatibility. So, after 24 h the cell viability remained over 80% for both of the carriers. Thus the prepared microspheres could be proposed as a safe carrier for drug delivery applications. On the other hand, as is seen in Fig. 9b, both the CUR solution and CUR-loaded microsphere exhibited a dose-dependent manner for cytotoxicity, and the high concentration of the active drugs in the free or microsphere loaded form led to the more killing of MDA-MB 231 cell. Additionally, the cytotoxicity of the free CUR solution was higher than Fe3O4/GQDs@GM@CUR and it was higher than GM@CUR. Similar results were reported by previous studies [79–81] that may be related to its sustained release profile from the drug-loaded microspheres and is in accordance with drug release results. According to the above results in the studied concentration range the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM could be introduced as a CUR delivery agent without any concerns about its toxicity.

In this study, the DAPI staining test was performed to further support the MTT test evidence. In this regard, the change in the chromatin density and its morphology was recorded by fluorescent microscopy after 24 h incubation of the treated MDA-MB 231 cells by free and dug loaded carriers, as well as, free CUR drug. From Fig. 10 it is showable that the GM and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM treated cells have no changes in chromatin morphology and their nucleus are like the control nucleus (As homogeneous blue spherical). Additionally, the nucleus intensity corresponding to the cell number for the treated MDA-MB 231 with GM and Fe3O4/GQDs@GM are similar to the untreated cells. The mentioned observations could be related to the nontoxic effect of each microsphere to the studied cell line. However, after treating with CUR and CUR-loaded carriers the condensation and chromatin fragmentation is visible which arises from the nucleus damaging as a result of CUR releases. Moreover for the CUR and CUR loaded carrier cell death can be detected after doing the DAPI staining test.

In this research, we presented the Fe3O4/GQDs nanohybrid coated with a pH-sensitive gelatin biopolymer as a newly developed carrier for hydrophobic anticancer drug delivery and compared its efficiency with pure GM. The Fe3O4 and GQDs separately were prepared and successfully hybridized. The FT-IR, EDX, and XRD spectra approved the preparation of Fe3O4/GQDs. Then, Fe3O4/GQDs were coated with gelatin. The successful formation of the ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@G hybrid microspheres certificated by FT-IR, PL, and SEM analysis. VSM and PL results showed that the prepared system can be proposed as a targeted carrier with the potential of imaging ability for future studies in the drug delivery area. In comparison with GM, the Fe3O4/GQDs@GM exhibited the more CUR loading capacity, as well as, higher CUR releases in the sustained and pH-sensitive manner. Furthermore, the MTT and DAPI staining assay exposed that the prepared microspheres could prove to be a safe carrier. The comparison of the drug delivery and biological test results showed improved performance for Fe3O4/GQDs@GM. The achieved results showed that the ternary Fe3O4/GQDs@G hybrid microsphere could be utilized as a biodegradable CUR delivery system to the cancer cells by potential target placing them in the vicinity of the tumor tissue and bioimaging capability.
Malihe Pooresmaeil: Writing - original draft, Methodology. Hassan Namazi: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (Grant Number 948645103) and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, the Tabriz University of Medical Science for their financial support of this research.
